text: The video shows a straightforward indirect inguinal hernia, clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies various preperitoneal pockets within the flap during the inguinal hernia repair.
The surgical view focuses on the parietal compartment medially, highlighting the anatomical area relevant to the inguinal hernia repair.
The surgeon begins dissection laterally to access the visceral compartment, following the identification of the hernia in the parietal compartment.
The surgeon addresses minor bleeding on the right side of the surgical field, preparing to manage it.
The surgeon works to maintain a very thin flap laterally, ensuring precise dissection and exposure of the underlying structures.
The surgeon addresses bleeding within the surgical field, ensuring hemostasis before proceeding further.
The surgeon continues to work, identifying and recognizing a distinct tissue layer during the procedure.
The surgeon maintains dissection within the current tissue layer, which results in the medialization of the cord element.
The surgeon enters the visceral compartment to efficiently dissect the peritoneal flap, ensuring precise separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon continues dissection in the parietal compartment, focusing on the retropubic space to create a flat area suitable for mesh placement.
The surgeon reduces a small hernia sac, preparing to shift focus to the next step in the procedure.
The surgeon performs parietalization of the cord structures, establishing the critical view of the myopectenial orifice, ensuring clear visualization of anatomical landmarks.
The surgeon performs a retropubic dissection, creating at least two centimeters of space between the bladder and the pubis, ensuring adequate separation for subsequent surgical steps.
The surgeon identifies the midline, marking a reference point for further dissection.
The surgeon examines the hernia spaces, including the femoral, obturator, indirect, and direct spaces, assessing their anatomical locations and relationships.
The surgeon demonstrates parietalization by reflecting the peritoneum downward, exposing the underlying vascular structures.
The surgeon parietalizes the vas by manipulating the medial umbilical ligament, reflecting the peritoneum to expose the vascular structures.
The surgeon distinguishes the fatty tissue from the femoral lymph nodes, noting its independence from these structures.
The surgeon works around the obturator space, manipulating the surrounding tissue to expose anatomical structures within this area.
The surgeon creates a clear and defined space within the surgical field, likely continuing the process of parietalizing vascular structures by reflecting the peritoneum.
The surgeon prepares the surgical site to accommodate the mesh, ensuring adequate space within the previously dissected area.
The surgeon identifies the obliterated umbilical artery, visually distinguishing it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon decides not to transect the fatty tissue, leaving it intact as it does not aid in developing an appropriate surgical pocket.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection around the obturator space, carefully separating tissue layers in this region.
The surgeon visually inspects the surgical field to confirm the absence of a cord lipoma.
The surgeon identifies the transversalis sling, ensuring no lipomas are present, then exchanges the scissors for a mega suture cut to place the mesh and close the flap.
The surgeon places the mesh over the surgical site and secures it with sutures at the beginning of the procedure.
The surgeon positions the mesh within the surgical site, ensuring it is appropriately aligned and secured.
The surgeon displays a mid-weight polypropylene mesh, preparing it for placement in the surgical field.
The flap and mesh are visibly moving within the surgical pocket.
The surgeon begins flap closure from lateral to medial, ensuring the medial aspect of the flap, which has no redundancy, is closed last to prevent excessive tension during lateral closure.
